sections.heading: Definition and Histology
sections.content: Occult primary cancer, also known as cancer of unknown primary (CUP), is defined as histologically confirmed metastatic tumors for which the primary site cannot be identified after standard pretreatment evaluation [1,2]. These tumors are epithelial in origin (carcinomas) and are characterized by early dissemination, aggressive behavior, and an unpredictable pattern of metastasis [3].

sections.heading: Epidemiology and Metastatic Sites
sections.content: Common sites of metastasis include the liver, lungs, bones, and lymph nodes [18,19,20]. CUP accounts for approximately 2% to 9% of all cancer diagnoses and is among the 10 most frequently diagnosed tumors in developed countries [2,8,9].

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.content: The diagnosis is made after ruling out other possibilities such as lymphoma, melanoma, sarcoma, and germ cell tumors.

sections.heading: Prognosis and Natural History
sections.content: CUP is associated with a poor prognosis, with a median overall survival of 8 to 12 months for most patients, although select patients with favorable subsets may have a median survival of 12 to 36 months [2,4,5]. In 20% to 50% of patients, the primary tumor is not identified even after postmortem examination [8,15,16]. A latent primary cancer may emerge during the natural course of the disease, although this is uncommon.
